title: Dawgs Of War
content:
آ آ آ آ Comes the British bulldog first, solid as a log,
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s so ugly in repose that heï؟½s a handsome dog;
آ آ آ آ Full of mild benevolence as his years increase;
آ آ آ آ Silent as a china dog on the mantelpiece.
آ آ آ آ Rub his sides and point his nose,
آ آ آ آ Click your tongue and in he goes,
آ آ آ آ To the thick of Britainï؟½s foes,
آ آ آ آ Enemies behind him close,

آ آ آ آ (Silence for a while.)

آ آ آ آ Comes a very different dog, tell him at a glance.
آ آ آ آ Clipped and trimmed and frilled all round. Dandy dog of France.
آ آ آ آ (Always was a dandy dog, no matter what his age)
آ آ آ آ Now his every hair and frill is stiff as wire with rage.
آ آ آ آ Rub his sides and point his nose,
آ آ آ آ Click your tongue and in he goes,
آ آ آ آ While behind him Franceï؟½s foes
آ آ آ آ Reel and surge and pack and close.

آ آ آ آ (Silence for a while.)

آ آ آ آ Next comes Belgiumï؟½s market dog, hard to realise.
آ آ آ آ Go-cart dog and barrow dog, heï؟½s a great surprise.
آ آ آ آ Dog that never hurt a cat, did no person harm;
آ آ آ آ Friendly, kindly, round and fat as a ï؟½Johnny Darm.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Rub his sides and point his nose,
آ آ آ آ Click your tongue and in he goes,
آ آ آ آ At the flank of Belgiumï؟½s foes
آ آ آ آ Who could not behind him close,

آ آ آ آ (Silence for a while.)

آ آ آ آ Next comes Serviaï؟½s mongrel pup, mongrel dawgs can fight;
آ آ آ آ Up or down, or down or up, whether wrong or right.
آ آ آ آ He was mad the other day, he is mad today,
آ آ آ آ Hustling round and raising dust in his backyard way.
آ آ آ آ Rub his sides and point his nose,
آ آ آ آ Click your tongue and in he goes,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twixt the legs of Serviaï؟½s foes,
آ آ آ آ Biting tails and rearmost toes,

آ آ آ آ (Silence for a while.)

آ آ آ آ There are various terrier dawgs mixed up in the scrap,
آ آ آ آ Much too small for us to see, and too mad to yap.
آ آ آ آ Each one, on his frantic own, heard the row commence,
آ آ آ آ Tore with tooth and claw a hole in the backyard fence.
آ آ آ آ No one called, but in they go,
آ آ آ آ Dogs with many a nameless woe,
آ آ آ آ Tripping up their common foe,

آ آ آ آ (Silence for a while.)

آ آ آ آ From the snows of Canada, dragging box and bale,
آ آ آ آ Comes the sledge-dog toiling on, sore-foot from the trail.
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½ll be useful in the trench, when the nose is blue,
آ آ آ آ Winter dog that knows the French and the English too.
آ آ آ آ Rub his sides and point his nose,
آ آ آ آ Click your tongue and in he goes,
آ آ آ آ At his fatherï؟½s countryï؟½s foes,
آ آ آ آ And his motherï؟½s countryï؟½s foes.

آ آ آ آ (Silence for a while.)

آ آ آ آ See, in sunny Southern France a dog that runs by sight,
آ آ آ آ Lean and yellow, sharp of nose, long of leg and light,
آ آ آ آ Silent and bloodthirsty, too; Distance in his eyes,
آ آ آ آ Leaping high to gain his view, the Kangaroo Dog flies!
آ آ آ آ Rub his sides and point his nose,
آ آ آ آ Click your tongue and up he goes,
آ آ آ آ Lands amongst his countryï؟½s foes,
آ آ آ آ And his countryï؟½s countryï؟½s foes;
آ آ آ آ While they sway and while they close,

آ آ آ آ (Silence for a while.)

آ آ آ آ See across the early snow, far across the plain,
آ آ آ آ Where the clouds are grey and low and winter comes again;
آ آ آ آ By the sand-dune and the marsh, and forest black and dumb,
آ آ آ آ As dusky white as their winterï؟½s night, the Russian wolf-hounds come!

آ آ آ آ (Silence for a while.)
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
